narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 55.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance. Your LSAT score of 28 also played a big role, as it's notably lower than the average of around 37 that we see in successful applicants. On top of that, your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 is below the dataset average of 3.21, and your middle family income bracket didn't provide the same boost as higher incomes often do. 

Among all these factors, your third-year decile ranking had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward predicting failure, followed closely by your first-year decile, then your LSAT score. Your family income and racial background as Asian came next in importance, both nudging the prediction toward a higher risk of failing the bar, with your undergraduate GPA rounding out the top influences in that direction. Being a full-time student had a minor push toward failure as well, while your gender as female was the one factor that slightly favored a passing prediction, though its impact was the smallest overall. 

Compared to typical students in our dataset—who are mostly white, full-time, and from higher income brackets with stronger grades and test scores—your profile stands out as riskier primarily due to those early academic struggles and lower standardized test performance. The good news is that some of these can be addressed: consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, as that's a key changeable factor that carries a lot of weight. In the meantime, focus on building stronger study habits or seeking tutoring to improve your performance trajectory, which could help demonstrate growth for future applications. With targeted preparation for the bar exam itself, like practice tests and courses, you could shift the odds in your favor next time around. Keep pushing—you have the determination to turn this around.
